div.p: In the Government of Canada, SSC's cloud framework agreement dictates cloud spending requires a Cloud Business Requirement (CBR) number.
The bureaucratic overhead/turnaround time and spending limits associated with a CBR require care to ensure service delivery isn't negatively affected.
The following approach is designed to support experimentation and rapid onboarding of teams in support of the "product team" model.

ul.li: These represent hands-on experiments and evaluations aimed at building knowledge/skills.

Isolated spaces (dedicated subscriptions) allows for enablement and live data
(live data is required for AI/ML experimentation).

CBR strategy:
Drawing down on a shared CBR reduces bureaucratic overhead to experimentation and enables rapid intake.

div.h2: Stream 2: Capability development: Alpha, Beta, Live

ul.li:
Three "production" environments based on team/product maturity levels:
Alpha: Polished subset of eventual functionality, low operational maturity (eg: uptime, respect for data contracts); Try, don't rely.
Beta: Expanded functionality, higher operational maturity (eg: have stated uptime objectives, might not meet them); Use, but excuse.
Live: Functionality tracks evolving user needs, high operational maturity; this is a service others can rely on.
Define graduation criteria upfront, move when maturity markers are achieved.

CBR strategy:
Rapid intake of Alpha/Beta workloads into shared CBR.
Workloads/costs distributed across Alpha/Beta CBRs.
Graduation to Beta kicks off background process to create a dedicated CBR for Live.
Capability teams shielded from CBR process.

Security boundaries create autonomy needed for capability delivery:
Limit blast radius with dedicated projects/accounts: insulates organization/others from team failure/compromise.
With limited blast radius, we can safely increase privilege inside projects/accounts: creates enabling environment supporting team autonomy.

Contrast:
"dev/test/prod": building trust in code.
"alpha/beta/live": building trust in teams.
